name: Fred Watkin
headline: Forensic Accountant & Researcher in Financial Crime | Specializing in Tax Appeals, Fraud Detection & Asset Tracing | Quadrant Forensics
text:
1. I do not work Sundays. Period.
2. Saturday is by appointment only and is half day only.
3. Clients who do not bring tax stuff in before April 20 have no guarantee of being filed on Apr 30.
4. I go out for lunch every day in tax season to get a break from work.
5. Sign efile authorization. Pay the
llm_reasoning: Forensic accountant describing their own firm's operational rules and workflow boundaries during tax season — clear small practice owner managing client load manually.

name: Karolyn Diaz, CPA, MBA
headline: PwC Alum | Serving Real Estate Industry & Professional Services clients with Bookkeeping, CFO-Advisory & Tax Strategy to Drive Cash Flow Decisions
text: Thank you for sharing! I just reposted. As tempting as it is, saying yes to new clients when there are a lot of unknowns right before a deadline can come back to bite ya!

name: Kory Reynolds
headline: Tax Principal at Baker Newman Noyes | Real Estate & Construction Industry Specialist | Top 100 Accounting Firm
text:
A very important rule in my book - start reaching to clients in very early January (if not earlier) to accelerate as much work as possible into January.

If we are able to get 40 hours a week of productivity starting mid January, that is 80-100+ hours you aren't stacking on top of already busy weeks
llm_reasoning: Tax Principal at Baker Newman Noyes, a Top 100 accounting firm — well outside the 3-15 person ICP, giving management advice not describing small-firm pain.

name: Rob Tamburri, CPA/PFS
headline: Managing Partner at Balog + Tamburri, Certified Public Accountants
text: If we need to extend your 3/15 deadline returns your 1040 is automatically extended.   Period
llm_reasoning: Managing Partner at a named CPA firm describing their own deadline extension policy — title confirms small firm operator role with hands-on workflow management.

name: Bill Bourbonnais, EA, MBA, CTC, CTS
headline: Strategic Tax Planning for Real Estate Investors & Business Owners | Find the Money You’re Overpaying | Enrolled Agent, MBA, Certified Tax Strategist
text:
Great list! Though I typically cut #1 by 20%. 3 is a lifesaver and a critical part of managing #1. 7 and 8 have been non-negotiable in nearly 30 years. For the first time in 2 decades, this year will see #6 implemented. 

Thanks for sharing!
llm_reasoning: Enrolled Agent with 30 years experience commenting on work-life balance rules — likely solo or very small practice, comment is reflective rather than expressing operational frustration.

name: Paul Medina
headline: CPA - Owner of Medina Company, PSC
text:
When I went self employed, my first rule was that I would try my darndest to not work any Sunday, even April. In 8 years, I've only worked two of them. 

I eat at my desk, but I turn on some sort of streaming service so my brain disengages completely - usually something I've watched before so I don'
llm_reasoning: CPA and Owner of Medina Company describing their own self-employment workflow habits — small firm owner managing tax season workload manually.

headline: International Speaker| EA Exam Prep Teacher| Digital Assets
text: These are important for a balanced tax season and balanced life. Those that think it can’t be achieved need to think of ways they can start to implement some of the things that seem out of reach. I do all of these and just made a decision to absolutely not eat at my desk this year.

name: Timalyn Bowens EA
headline: Tax Expert, Enrolled Agent, trainer, and writer empowering taxpayers to take their life back from IRS back taxes. Host of the Tax Relief with Timalyn Bowens Podcast.
text:
My biggest regrets are #6 and #8. Seriously, work can wait 30 minutes for us to eat. 

#8 - Instead of going the BU Men's championship game I was sitting in the office kitchen/common space with my laptop, wishing I was there. 

I was so focused on making a good impression that I missed out on a once
